lg.l: maṉpatai coṉṉa tevaṉvāḻi yōtōtañcamō tōḻī talaivaṉ varakkāṇēṉ
As the graceful lady who danced - the elderly cowherdress woman - sprinkling,
While praising the feet of Neṭumāl [at] the Vaiyai river [with] long[-lasting] waters
Flowers, incense, sandal paste for applying [on the body] and a chaplet [as offerings],
She who went in order to bathe at the ghat having [now] reached [the village],
Hearing a bustling sound of the village, at the end of the Kuravai dance,
She, indeed, did not engage in speech. She stood [there], not speaking.
To that lady, [Kaṇṇaki said], ‘Speak, please speak! I did not see my husband.
Besides [this], my heart sighs, so that the blowing fire-bellows are defeated [to it].
Since my heart sighs, so that the blowing fire-bellows are defeated,
Oh friend, pray, what was said [by] the strangers?
Even in the good day time, [my] tremble[-inducing] pain increases.
Besides [this], my heart is troubled, not having seen [my] beloved man.
Since my heart is troubled, not having seen [my] beloved man,
Oh friend, pray, what was said [by] the people?
Is it a negligble matter, oh friend? I don’t see the hero [Kōvalaṉ] coming!
Besides — there is an [act of] deceit [= something is wrong]. My heart swoons!
There is [an act of] deceit. Since my heart swoons,
Pray, friend, what was said [by] the strangers?’
That which was said [by the messenger girl, from the report of strangers]’:
Saying, there is a thief who silently took an ornamental anklet
[From] the palace - the residence of the king. Saying,
There is a thief who silently took [it]. [Those] people of the tinkling anklet
She got up, bursting [with emotion]. She fell [to the ground],
As though the moon [with] showering rays along with all the clouds
Took to [= fell to] the ground in a distance. Crying, so that [her] eyes reddened,
She would be lost [in her grief], lamenting in agony, [saying] to her husband
‘Like the ladies filled with misery, having practised austeries [because of]
The forms of distress [of being widowed], [or as other such women] dive into
Painful flames [because of the death of] joyful husbands,
I have lost [my] beloved man, as the king committed an error
As the people spread gossip [that it was me].
Will I, having taken on misery, [also] be desolate?
Like miserable women, having lost [their] male companion
With a fragrant, abundantly wide chest, lamenting, bathing in many ghats,
As the king who roams about with the sceptre of valour committed an error,
Oh Fool who is called righteousness! Will, having taken on misery, be desolate?
Like the distressed [= widowed] women, abundant in [their] widowhood, who dive into ghats,
[Or other such women] dive into fiery flames of the great husband they [once] possessed,
Will I, having despaired [and] lamented, be desolate even in this birth,
As the southern One [with] a sceptre that was distant from impartiality committed an error?
Don’t you see? All of you foolish women [in each other’s] company that come together
Having come from the [dancing of] the Kuravai that occured with excellence,
Listen! All of you foolish women [in each other’s] company, please listen!
You may know the matters that happen [in the world with] a fence of spreading waves —
Oh Lord [with] scorching rays! Is my husband a thief?
One voice said, ‘Oh lady [with] eyes [like] black carps! He is not a thief.
This town will be consumed by bright flames.’
